ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general coffee beverage class; no specific mocha subclass exists. Closest defensible parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers coffee-based beverages broadly; no mocha-specific code exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for mocha beverage identified in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard medium serving of mocha is approximately 200 ml (200 g), delivering around 136 kcal, 16.6 g sugar, and 4.6 g protein per cup.

nutrient_source.title: Mocha — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (D09010046)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record D09010046 for Mocha (beverage made with steamed full cream milk, espresso and chocolate syrup). All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk-based coffee beverages — analogous estimate based on full-cream milk GI (27) and chocolate-flavoured milk GI (34–43) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for mocha exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 41 based on the closest analogous foods: full-cream milk (GI ~27, University of Sydney) and chocolate-flavoured milk beverages (GI ~34–43, University of Sydney). The dominant carbohydrate source is lactose from milk and sucrose from chocolate syrup; the blended GI is estimated at 41 (low category). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (41 × 9.5 × 2) / 100 = 7.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.3 g/100 g) in a beverage exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold; added sugar independently raises blood pressure via insulin-driven sodium retention.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or limit mocha due to its 8.3 g sugar per 100 g; request sugar-free chocolate syrup and unsweetened milk, or substitute with plain espresso or Americano, per AHA 2018 guidelines on sugar and blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.3 g sugar per 100 g; a standard 200 ml serving delivers approximately 16.6 g sugar, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid regular mocha; if consumed, request sugar-free chocolate syrup and unsweetened milk, limit to a small serving, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as liquid sugars are absorbed faster than solid carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Contains saturated fat from full cream milk (~1.32 g/100 g) and added sugar (8.3 g/100 g); regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit mocha consumption due to combined saturated fat and high added sugar content; choose a smaller serving with reduced-fat milk and sugar-free chocolate syrup, or opt for plain black coffee to minimise cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (8.3 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators, negating the anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit of cocoa.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sweetened mocha; an unsweetened dark cocoa drink prepared with unsweetened cocoa powder and no added sugar would be preferable to leverage cocoa flavanols without the inflammatory burden of added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mocha is a popular espresso-based coffee beverage made by combining a shot of espresso with steamed full cream milk and chocolate syrup, resulting in a rich, creamy drink with a sweet chocolate-coffee flavour profile. The beverage is served hot or iced and is widely available in cafés globally, including Singapore. Per 100 g, mocha provides 68 kcal, 2.29 g protein, 2.13 g fat, 9.5 g carbohydrates, and 8.3 g sugar, with 32 mg sodium and 50 mg calcium. The relatively high sugar content (8.3 g/100 g) classifies mocha as a sugar-sweetened beverage, making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without modification. The chocolate syrup contributes both sugar and a small amount of dietary fibre (1.44 g/100 g) from cocoa solids. Clinically, the liquid sugar matrix means rapid glucose absorption, and regular consumption is associated with elevated triglycerides and blood pressure. Healthier modifications include using sugar-free chocolate syrup, reduced-fat milk, and smaller serving sizes. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 41 (low category) based on analogous milk-chocolate beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a mocha?
What is the difference between mocha and latte?
How much sugar is in a mocha coffee?
Is mocha a good choice for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record D09010046 at confidence 0.95, which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 1.32 g/100 g based on typical full cream milk contribution (approximately 62% of total fat as saturated fat per USDA SR Legacy for whole milk); this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB source. Fields set to -1 (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) are not reported in the HPB Foodo D09010046 record and detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific mocha preparation are not available in the HPB database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. GI value of 41 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for mocha exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input are retained as contextual tags only; 'low_calorie' is borderline at 68 kcal/100 g and should be reviewed against Singapore HPB low-calorie beverage thresholds. The mocha is not classified as a Singaporean dish by origin; multilingual names are provided as internationally used terms for this globally recognised beverage.
